title: Northern Hemisphere

infobox.key: About two-thirds, from north of [Libreville](/wiki/Libreville "Libreville") in Gabon in the west to south of [Mogadishu](/wiki/Mogadishu "Mogadishu") in Somalia in the east.
infobox.value: The entire continental mainland. Part of Indonesia, and [24 out of 26 atolls](/wiki/Atolls_of_the_Maldives "Atolls of the Maldives") of Maldives in the Indian Ocean, are also in the Northern Hemisphere. | All of North America, Central America, and the [Caribbean](/wiki/Caribbean "Caribbean") islands. About one-fifth of South America, from north of [Quito](/wiki/Quito "Quito") in Ecuador in the west to north of the [Amazon River](/wiki/Amazon_River "Amazon River") mouth in Brazil in the east. | Entirely in the Northern Hemisphere[[note 1]](#cite_note-overseas-europe-15)

is the half of Earth that is north of the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). For other planets in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), north is defined as being in the same [celestial hemisphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere") relative to the [invariable plane](/wiki/Invariable_plane "Invariable plane") of the Solar System as Earth's North Pole.[[1]](#cite_note-report-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Half of Earth that is north of the Equator

This article is about the northern half of planet Earth. For use of the term describing astronomical observations, see [Northern celestial hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_celestial_hemisphere "Northern celestial hemisphere").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Hemisferio_Norte.png/250px-Hemisferio_Norte.png)](/wiki/File:Hemisferio_Norte.png)

Location of the Northern Hemisphere

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Northern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png/250px-Northern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_Hemisphere_LamAz.png)

Northern Hemisphere from above the North Pole

The **Northern Hemisphere** is the half of Earth that is north of the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"). For other planets in the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), north is defined as being in the same [celestial hemisphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere") relative to the [invariable plane](/wiki/Invariable_plane "Invariable plane") of the Solar System as Earth's North Pole.[[1]](#cite_note-report-1)

Due to Earth's [axial tilt](/wiki/Axial_tilt "Axial tilt") of 23.439281°, there is a seasonal variation in the lengths of the day and night. There is also a seasonal variation in temperatures, which lags the variation in day and night. Conventionally, winter in the Northern Hemisphere is taken as the period from the [December solstice](/wiki/December_solstice "December solstice") (typically December 21 [UTC](/wiki/UTC "UTC")) to the [March equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox") (typically March 20 UTC), while summer is taken as the period from the [June solstice](/wiki/June_solstice "June solstice") through to the [September equinox](/wiki/September_equinox "September equinox") (typically on 23 September UTC). The dates vary each year due to the difference between the [calendar year](/wiki/Calendar_year "Calendar year") and the [astronomical year](/wiki/Year#Astronomical_years "Year"). Within the Northern Hemisphere, oceanic currents can change the weather patterns that affect many factors within the north coast. Such events include [El Niño–Southern Oscillation](/wiki/El_Ni%C3%B1o%E2%80%93Southern_Oscillation "El Niño–Southern Oscillation").

[Trade winds](/wiki/Trade_winds "Trade winds") blow from east to west just above the equator. The winds pull surface water with them, creating currents, which flow westward due to the [Coriolis effect](/wiki/Coriolis_effect "Coriolis effect"). The currents then bend to the right, heading north. At about 30 degrees north latitude, a different set of winds, the [westerlies](/wiki/Westerlies "Westerlies"), push the currents back to the east, producing a closed clockwise loop.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Its surface is 60.7% water, compared with 80.9% water in the case of the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), and it contains 67.3% of Earth's land.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The continents of North America and mainland [Eurasia](/wiki/Eurasia "Eurasia") are located entirely in the Northern Hemisphere, together with about two-thirds of Africa and a small part of South America.

## Geography and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Hemisphere&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography and climate")]

During the 2.5 million years of the [Pleistocene](/wiki/Pleistocene "Pleistocene"), numerous cold phases called [glacials](/wiki/Glacial_period "Glacial period") ([Quaternary ice age](/wiki/Quaternary_glaciation "Quaternary glaciation")), or significant advances of continental ice sheets, in Europe and North America, occurred at intervals of approximately 40,000 to 100,000 years. The long glacial periods were separated by more temperate and shorter [interglacials](/wiki/Interglacial "Interglacial") which lasted about 10,000–15,000 years. The last cold episode of the [last glacial period](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period") ended about 10,000 years ago.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Earth is currently in an interglacial period of [the Quaternary](/wiki/Quaternary "Quaternary"), called the [Holocene](/wiki/Holocene "Holocene").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The glaciations that occurred during the glacial period covered many areas of the Northern Hemisphere.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Northern_icesheet_hg.png/250px-Northern_icesheet_hg.png)](/wiki/File:Northern_icesheet_hg.png)

Northern Hemisphere glaciation during the last [ice ages](/wiki/Ice_age "Ice age"). The setup of 3 to 4-kilometer thick ice sheets caused a [sea level lowering](/wiki/Sea_level_rise "Sea level rise") of about 120 m.

The [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") is a region around the North Pole (90° [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude")). Its climate is characterized by cold winters and cool summers. Precipitation mostly comes in the form of snow. Areas inside the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle") (66°34′ latitude) experience some days in summer when the Sun never sets, and some days during the winter when it never rises. The duration of these phases varies from one day for locations right on the Arctic Circle to several months near the Pole, which is the middle of the Northern Hemisphere. Between the Arctic Circle and the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") (23°26′ latitude) lies the [Northern temperate zone](/wiki/Northern_temperate_zone "Northern temperate zone"). The changes in these regions between summer and winter are generally mild, rather than extreme hot or cold. However, a temperate climate can have very unpredictable weather.

Tropical regions (between the Tropic of Cancer and the Equator, 0° latitude) are generally hot all year round and tend to experience a [rainy season](/wiki/Wet_season "Wet season") during the summer months, and a [dry season](/wiki/Dry_season "Dry season") during the winter months.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/Snow_covered_mountains_in_Mount_Robson_%28Unsplash%29.jpg/250px-Snow_covered_mountains_in_Mount_Robson_%28Unsplash%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Snow_covered_mountains_in_Mount_Robson_(Unsplash).jpg)

[Canadian Rockies](/wiki/Canadian_Rockies "Canadian Rockies") in North America

In the Northern Hemisphere, objects moving across or above the surface of the Earth tend to turn to the right because of the [Coriolis effect](/wiki/Coriolis_effect "Coriolis effect"). As a result, large-scale horizontal flows of air or water tend to form clockwise-turning [gyres](/wiki/Ocean_gyre "Ocean gyre").[[6]](#cite_note-USDoC-6) These are best seen in ocean circulation patterns in the [North Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") and [North Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Ocean "Pacific Ocean") oceans.[[6]](#cite_note-USDoC-6) Within the Northern Hemisphere, oceanic currents can change the weather patterns that affect many factors within the north coast.[[7]](#cite_note-7) For the same reason, flows of air down toward the northern surface of the Earth tend to spread across the surface in a clockwise pattern. Thus, clockwise air circulation is characteristic of [high pressure](/wiki/High_pressure "High pressure") weather cells in the Northern Hemisphere. Conversely, air rising from the northern surface of the Earth (creating a region of low pressure) tends to draw air toward it in a counterclockwise pattern. [Hurricanes](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") and [tropical storms](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") (massive low-pressure systems) spin counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The shadow of a sundial moves clockwise on latitudes north of the [subsolar point](/wiki/Subsolar_point "Subsolar point") and anticlockwise to the south. During the day at these latitudes, the Sun tends to rise to its maximum at a southerly position. Between the Tropic of Cancer and the Equator, the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun") can be seen to the north, directly overhead, or to the south at noon, depending on the time of year. In the Southern Hemisphere, the midday Sun is predominantly in the north.

When viewed from the Northern Hemisphere, the [Moon](/wiki/Moon "Moon") appears inverted compared to a view from the Southern Hemisphere.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) The North Pole faces away from the [Galactic Center](/wiki/Galactic_Center "Galactic Center") of the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way"). This results in the Milky Way being sparser and dimmer in the Northern Hemisphere compared to the Southern Hemisphere, making the Northern Hemisphere more suitable for deep-space observation, as it is not "blinded" by the Milky Way.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[11]](#cite_note-11)

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Northern_Hemisphere&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Demographics")]

As of 2015, the Northern Hemisphere is home to approximately 6.4 billion people, which is around 87.0% of the Earth's total human population of 7.3 billion people.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14)
